Oren: So clearly the live action fire you see a real pitch, you see a pitch
that's not as packaged as the package presentation, but you see what's
really working. Now, this is more live action fire. I sit on the intake
committee of a family office, about an $80 million fund, family office.
Whenever they get a deal that they're interested in, they send it over to
me and I look at it, and I say, "Guys I like it," and then if we're interested
they bring in the company and they pitch us, so I see pitches all the time.
I sit on the intake committee at a hedge fund, controls about $100 million
worth of investment capital, and I probably know $5 or $6 billion of capital
that's out there, so when people come to me I really get deals financed.
It's what I do, okay? Being a book writer or speaking on stage, as you can
see, is not my day to get grind; getting deals financed. So I thought I would
hear from you a little bit about your deal and then reflect back to you a
different way of pitching it, the way I would like to be pitched it. So tell me
a little bit about yourself and your company.
William: Thank you for choosing me. My name is William Martin. The
company is United Mines out of Oro Valley, Arizona. It's a publicly traded
company with the stock symbol UNMN. The business is about 8 years old,
it's been public for about 2 years now. It's a real deal and it's got a pretty
interesting history. In the mid-1800s it was owned by Samuel Colt from
Colt Firearms, and at the time it was the largest producing silver mine in
the world, and it was last in production in the early 70s. But we're trying
to raise $2-4 million right now to put it back into production. There are
approximately 300,000-500,000 ounces of silver sitting on top of the
ground in mine dump tailings that they didn't have the technology to
process and extract the silver at the time, and plus silver was too cheap,
now that it's up around $30 an ounce it makes a lot more sense. And the
people behind it have combined about 200 years of mining experience.
Oren: Let me ask you this: what is your business model?
William: The business model is to raise the money, put it back into
production, and put drills into the ground to further explore. There's $3-5
billion inferred, but we need to get in there and prove it.
Pitch Teardown
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	1
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 15 --

Oren: All right. What's the problem?
William: The problem is that probably we're too small for the biggies, and
it's been difficult to get out and get in front of the right people and, as you
know, there' s a million deals out there.
Oren: So what problem does the company solve? Why don't other people
do this? Why is this hard to do?
William: Raising money is...
Oren: No, no, no, the company. The actual extraction.
William: Well, it's not necessarily all that hard to do, but it takes money.
Oren: Okay, so what's the use of funds when you raise the money?
William: The first money will go into extracting the silver that's in the mine
dump tailings, that's sitting above ground as we speak.
Oren: And then what's the gross margin? What are the economics? So
you spend $1 million and what happens?
William: Well the return right off the $1 million should return somewhere
between $9-$15 million, just from the silver that's in the mine dump
tailings, and that will give more than enough money to get the drills in the
ground to prove out the reserves, the inferred reserves.
Oren: Got it. So, let me reflect back a way that I think this could be done
very powerfully. So when I ask someone, "What's your business model?",
this is what I'm looking for, especially in this situation: "What's your
business model?" Many people think, you say "What's your business
model?" "Oh, well, we buy a widget, we add some value to it, we mark it
up, we put a label on it, and we sell it. So our business is that we are a
value-added reseller, that's how we make our margin."
That's not a business model. If you go to Harvard or Yale, and you take a
class with Michael Porter on business models, this is what he'll say: "What
do you do yourself and what do you outsource, what do you not do, first
off.
Pitch Teardown
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	2
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 15 --

Second, who are your customers, and which customers in your market
will let you profit the most from them? Somebody should write this down
because I do not have a slide on this. So what do you do and what do you
not do? Who are your target customers, and who will let you profit the
most from them? How do you get into the sales channel, how do you go
to market, how do you get in the channel? How do you capture value as
part of the value that you provide? How do you charge? How do you
capture value as part of the value you provide, and how do you defend
your revenue streams from other people who want those same revenues?
So let's feed your deal through that model, alright? So what is it you do
specifically versus what you outsource?
William: Specifically, we mine. We mine the metal out, the ore out of the
ground, and then we can actually process it right on the ground. The
facility is fully permitted, a leach field, to extract the metals into a
concentrate and then we'll outsource the smelting of the concentrate.
Oren: Smelting, okay. What's hard about mining?
William: Well, digging holes. And the way this is done, it's not like the mines
are real deep and they can collapse, a lot of it's surface mining and
following veins down.
Oren: So this is not buying servers and making Facebook, this is physical
interaction with the Earth, and that's hard.
William: Correct.
Oren: All right. And dangerous.
William: Well, not so much. Not like it used to be.
Oren: So who are the customers? Because I think this is really important,
right? We heard a pitch here, but we didn't hear anything about the
customers yet. So who are the customers? And not only who are the
customers, but which customers will let us profit the most?
Pitch Teardown
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	3
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 15 --

William: Well the customer is anyone who believes in silver and thinks that
the price of silver is going to rise.
Oren: We can't have customers who are anyone.
William: Okay. I mean, at this point, really, the customers- We're looking
for some investors that are able to write a check for a substantial...
Oren: So the customers are investors.
William: Yes.
Oren: All right. Tell me a little bit about this customer. How big is the
check? Because we might find some check writers right here in a few
minutes.
William: I mean, in reality it would be nice to have like one or two to write a
check for like $2-$4 million, but I guess there's a few ways to do this. The
lowest amount of stock that the company has ever sold publicly was $1.25
a share. Right now it's trading around 50 cents. It came out at $5, but
since it's not in production it get's beaten down and what have you. It's
got probably the lowest float of any public company out there, there's
only like 15 million shares that are in circulation.
Oren: How do you get in the channel? How do you go out and find the
investors? Let's go back to the investors. Who's the perfect...because
there's a...anybody that has a deal in here there's 1,000 investors within a
100 miles of here, and investors...let's pause on this...investors segment
themselves into risk-reward buckets.
So I had a deal a few months ago, a guaranteed 20% return. Guaranteed.
Because a friend of mine had invested in a software company and needed
some more money, but his fund was at their limit for an individual
investment. So he says, "If you bring $1 million from one of your buddies,
we'll guarantee it with our assets. Guaranteed 20% return.
So I go to some of my venture buddies, and I say, "I got an easy one for
you. Guaranteed 20% return by the assets of a well-known hedge fund."
They say, "[Owen], that's great. 20% return is awesome. But we're a
venture group, we try to get 3-5x, we try to get a 10x on our money. Even
Pitch Teardown
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	4
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 15 --

if we get this 20%, our investors will be mad at us for returning 20%.
That's not we're in the business of getting. So as great as this is, you're
wasting your time with us. We can't take a 20% return.
William: We've heard that before.
Oren: Yeah, and now the other thing you'll hear, I go to other guys. "Hey,
guys I guarantee you a 20% return by the assets of a hedge fund. It's real."
"Owen, we'd love to get a 20% return, but in our business we dream of
getting 10s, we dream of hitting a 10 bogie, we dream of getting a 10%
annual return, and we know in reality we only get 7s for what we know, so
a 20% would make our investors and our partners very, very
uncomfortable, because it's completely outside what we think is
achievable. Investors have a very specific risk-reward appetite.
And so I said there's 1,000 investors within a 100 miles of here, I could
introduce you to 200 of them, but within 20 seconds of meeting them,
they're going to say, "We don't have a bucket for that. It's not in our risk-
return profile." So now we've got think about--and we may not be able to
do it here--who will let us profit? Who wants to, who has a bucket for
this? Who has this risk-return appetite?
So on here some people want to get very, very certain 10% yield, and on
the other side of the book-end, some people don't care about losing their
money, but they want a chance to make a 10 times return, and we're
somewhere in the middle, so we've got to find, we've got the filter the
market. And in any business there's all kinds of customers, but who will
let us profit from them? Who has a bucket for this product or this money?
So we've got to think more about who's the perfect customer, who's the
perfect investor. Do you have any sense of that?
William: The perfect investor is someone who's looking to make a nice
return but in a reasonable amount of time.
Oren: Okay, I got it. Next, let's talk about how you get into the channel,
how you're going out to find these investors or buyers. So just a quick
refresher, we've got to figure out these five things to be able to talk out
this business: What you do and what you don't do? We've got that
covered. Who are your ideal investors for it, what is their psychographic?

-- 5 of 15 --

And now we're talking about where do you find them. So where's the best
place to find investors for the deal? How do you get into the channel?
William: That's an excellent question. Because it's a publicly traded
company, one has to be careful about statements made, et cetera, so it's
been word of mouth and, I mean this is an awesome opportunity here to
get in front of a group like this.
Oren: Okay, so next, how do you make money in this? How do you make
money, how do you capture value as part of the value you give? I don't
think that was clear to all of us, so what is the specific, economic value
proposition?
William: I'm not quite sure how to answer that.
Oren: All right, so we extract it from the earth.
William: Yes.
Oren: We did something with it--We outsourced it to smelting, and then
we got some valuable mineral. So how much did we spend on doing that,
and what is it now worth?
William: What is spent is minimal, compared to what it's worth. What is
minimal? I believe it's around 10%. So, silver's around $30 an ounce right
now, so it's $3 to $4 to get it back into a form that's marketable.
Oren: So there might be an 80% or more gross margin on the product?
William: Correct. Yes.
Oren: That's how we capture value. So if we get a lot of investors and this
business is going well, why would people choose us over anyone else?
What unfair advantage do we have? Do we have mining licenses that
nobody else has? do we know how to sell it? What unfair advantage do we
have in this market?
William: That's an excellent question. We do have 3,000 acres of mines
permitted from the Bureau of Mining in the Federal Government.

-- 6 of 15 --

Oren: Okay, so we've got 3,000 acres that nobody else can take away
because we own them.
William: Correct.
Oren: So last, let's try and figure out. The last thing we want to try and
figure out here. We understand... So let's just run through it again. All
right, what's changing in this business. Let's figure out what's changing in
this business, why there's an opportunity here. What's making this
opportunity come to market? What's changing?
William: What's making this opportunity come to market is the need for
cash to get into production.
Oren: Okay. What's changing in the market for silver?
William: The price appears to be heading north.
Oren: Okay, the price is going north. Is there anything in the technology of
mining that's changing?
William: Technology continues to get better all the time.
Oren: Okay, I'm going to try to pitch this deal to you with what I know, so
let me have you take a seat back here, so you can watch it.
William: Okay. Alright. Thank you.
Oren: Thank you very much. That's awesome.
William: That's for you. Thank you.
Speaker: I love it. All right. Thank you very much for sharing that. My tiny
pea brain is moving a hundred miles an hour trying to figure this deal out.
That's that noise you hear. I want to tell you a little bit about our business.
We are a mining company. We do something very difficult. In today's
world you hear about lots of businesses, and they're on the front page of
Wired magazine, and the New York Times, and the Wall Street Journal, and
they buy servers, and they take them to a little server farm and they've got
lots of little 24-year-olds and 19-year-olds coding away, and they do
Pitch Teardown
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	7
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 15 --

engineering, and they release a product like Groupon. It all happens in air-
conditioned rooms up in Silicon Valley.
We're a different kind of business. We work in the physical world. We do
something very hard. We take heavy machinery and we apply it against
the earth and against the will of the earth. We extract massive amounts of
raw product, of raw material, and so just as hard of that is, challenging the
will of planet Earth, just knowing where to do this, is an information feat
beyond the scope of even an hour or two hours or weeks of description.
You need PhDs in mineralogy and analyzing satellite photos, and you need
to have...You can't be a 24-year-old, we don't have a single 24-year-old
in our company, except the guy who washes the dishes, okay?
This is guys, and if a guy comes in and he says...In the server business,
Groupon, they get a 50-year-old guy in that business, he's a senior
citizen, they wonder what he's doing there. In our business, a guy's 50-
years-old, we say, "Oh, you're just getting started." I'm not going to go dig
anywhere that somebody who's not 55 to 65 years old has told me to dig
because they need to have had 40 years in this business, because I won't
trust millions of dollars of equipment and men and people and lives to go
challenge the will of the planet, to go extract raw material.
But once we've done all this, it's incredibly hard work, we extract this
material and then we sort it all out, and then we send it to a smelter, he
returns the raw material to us, and we have this: and incredibly rare and
valuable element. And the way we do it, because we've been doing it for a
long time and we understand the technology, and we've got experienced
people.
Well the way we do this very difficult thing, we get an 80-90% gross
margin on this effort. So if we do it, and we do it as well as it can be done
we get a 90% gross margin, but on our worst day we get a 70% gross
margin, it's a very, very profitable business. Very profitable.
But things are changing in our market. If you don't understand these
changes, you would not want to participate, because gold, silver, and
copper, and commodities is a business that goes up and it goes down, and
if you think it's going up when it's going down, you're hosed, and we
would never want anybody to work with us to misunderstand what's
happening in this market.
Pitch Teardown
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	8
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 15 --

So you've got to understand three drivers of this business today. One is
the value of silver is trending up, and it has been trending, and the experts
who track commodities are telling us it's got another four or five years of
trending, and they're not wrong. You can look at their 50 years of
prediction, and they're not wrong about this stuff. The models are very
good, so you're going to see another four or five years of lift in the market.
Now we don't know exactly what day it's going to turn or when the lift is
going to go shallow, but it's not going to turn around tomorrow. These
markets are stable, they're not mercurial. They don't change on a dime.
In fact, you have a war in Iran, you know Israel drops the super-bomb on
Iran, and the price goes up, which hopefully will happen. So that's one
thing, is you can look at the trend lines and the models, and the trend line
is up.
The second thing that's happening, is because of the economic downturn,
more people are moving into hard commodities with their money, they
want that as a protective element. So you're seeing a social change in this
business, where before it just used to be for expert traders, but now, man
on the street, your average guy, you have financial managers
recommending these times of commodities as a safe haven.
So you've got a social change, and there's also a very strong technological
change, a very strong technological change happening in this business.
We just know how to mine better. The technology is getting better. We get
more extraction for less effort, and more money. So if you have a company
that knows how to apply the new technologies, you can have a significant
advantage.
So, the prices are trending up, and they will trend up for a while. The
models are good. You have to understand that. Number two, you
understand the social change which people are beginning to see this more
as a safe haven, it's becoming more acceptable. And three, the
technologies are getting better. This is opening a window of opportunity
that we're stepping through. Now other people do this.
Other people do this, and other people do this well. There are 10
companies in the market that do what we do. They own thousands of
acres, they apply their force of will against the earth with equipment, they
Pitch Teardown
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	9
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 15 --

have very seasoned professionals, and they extract this stuff from the
ground, and they use outsourced smelters, and they create incredibly
valuable commodity, and then they sell it in the market.
But we do something that's very special. We know where to dig better
than anyone else. That's where we invested a lot of our money. We know
where to dig better than anyone else. We have a team of analysis. We
spend more than anybody else.
It's interesting, I'll just pause on this for a minute, when America and
Russia were coming to power, what happened is that in the late '30s, early
'40s, America believed that micro-electronics were going to be the
dominant technology that would rule the political structure of the world,
and Russia believed that it was metallurgy. The better rockets, and
engines, and tanks, and guns that you could make with metal, that you
would be able to dominate the political structure of the world. But nobody
knew for sure then what the outcome would be.
So America chose microelectronics and we invested in that, and we
created Silicon Valley and we have MIT, and we have a huge amount of
government and company infrastructure around microelectronics, and
Russia focused their entire academic and governmental infrastructure on
getting great metallurgy.
Now today the United States of America buys rocket engines from Russia,
both technology and actual rocket engines. Their jet-fighters do things
that jet-fighters built by the United States of America can't do. So that
decision from 1930 to 1940 is active today. You don't think Russia wants
to be as good at microelectronics as we are? But they can't. You don't
think the United States wants to rely on Russia for rocket engines? We
want to build them better, but we can't, they're just better than us.
So what happens is, you pick as you go into a market, you pick a strategic
competitive advantage, and you focus on it, and today Russia dominates in
metallurgy, and their rocket engines, and their jet fighters, and the
things-- they can build things better than we can. And we have better
microelectronics, and we can build better servers, and we have Google.
But the point is, you can't channel hop. You can't just say, "Oh,
microelectronics turned out to be great. That was a good strategic choice,
Pitch Teardown
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	10
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 15 --

we're going to compete with you on it." So in our market we chose the
analytics of where to mine as our competitive strength, and we invested in
it more than other people did. You don't think other companies want to do
it as well as we can? Too bad. They need to get in a time machine, go back
15 years when we started doing it, and invest the way we did. Okay? Back
to the future. They can't, they cannot do it as well as us. We're the best at
doing that.
That's important. There's 10 of us, but we're the best, so we get the
highest margin, so if you're an investor in our deal, we get the highest
margin of any of the 10 competitors, because we are more efficient at
knowing where to apply our resources than the next guys, and it's very
important, because other people do what we do. Now there's other
people that are better at digging, we admit that. But we don't think
digging is the dominant skill in this market, we think knowing where to dig.
So we're pretty damn good at digging, but there's guys who are amazing at
it.
And as you see me here today, there's guys that are amazing at selling,
they suck at knowing where to dig, and they suck at digging, because they
buy old equipment and they don't apply technologies, and they've got lots
of young guys running around, it's a cool place to work, but their skill is
selling, they sell much harder than we do, but they don't make as much
money, they just sell harder. Our skill is in knowing where to dig, and
that's the dominant competitive advantage in the market.
So the market is changing, there's an opportunity, there's a window of
opportunity now to buy this stuff. We don't think that these forces are
going to hold. We think other companies are going to get these
technologies and learn how to apply them. We think the trend line is going
to change and flatten out. And we think that it's going to plateau, this
social interest, as the economy gets better, the social interest to buy silver
will flatten out as well, so there's a market window of opportunity now.
You couldn't do this a year ago.
You couldn't do this a year ago or two years ago because the technology
wasn't as good and the social desire for silver wasn't as strong, and you
might not be able to buy the way we can do it now two years from now, as
these trend lines flatten out.
Pitch Teardown
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	11
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 15 --

Now is the market, now is the window of opportunity. We're better than
our competitors, the price is at an all-time high, the forces are in our
direction, and if you were ever going to do this, it wasn't two years ago and
it's not two years from now, now is the time to do it.
I want to tell you a little bit about this business that I just learned about 5
minutes ago. I think I said to you what we do: we do this incredibly hard
work of knowing where to dig, we apply the equipment, we get the stuff
out of the mine, that's all of the hard work. We send it off to a smelter, the
reason we don't do that, that's a low-margin business, it's easy to do, it
requires very little technology, and we just outsource it. There's no value
in having that, so we do the hard work where we can get a real
competitive advantage.
We find investors who care about commodities, and maybe they're getting
a 40% or 50% IR in commodities, but how we find for our company that
we know a guy is right, is he's bought commodities, and he may even
bought gold, silver, copper before, and he's looking for the appreciation
bogie that we provide of a 80% return, and he's okay to take the risk.
Because every once in a while, we go to dig and we miss, and there's a
risk.
But the investor we're looking for that's perfect for us, and in fact, all of
you, if you can't stomach the risk, and you want an 8% return for your
money, you should go somewhere else. This is an 80% plus yield with an
appropriate amount of risk, but if you like that, you're perfect because
you'll like us as people, we're fair and we're honest and we're integral, and
we're transparent, and we give visibility, and we have a long track record,
and I will work very hard at every thing that I say I'm going to do.
So we're good guys to work with, you take some risk with us that's
appropriate, you get a 80% yield. But that's who's perfect for us. If you're
outside those bookends, you're not for us. You should go somewhere else,
and I'll tell you where else to go. You should go invest in Groupon, or go
buy a Triplex in Ocean Beach with tenants in it and make a 3% yield a year
by having a piece of real estate that you own.
We find our investors, the way we go to market, is at conferences. So
there's conferences that care about mining, and we go there and we make
a presentation, and some people are interested in the presentation
Pitch Teardown
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	12
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 15 --

because they care about how we apply technology, and they care about
the gross margin that we get, and they care about our track record over
time and our ability to do this.
And so, after we do a presentation at the conference, they come to us and
they say, "I loved your presentation. You guys seem more of an expert
than anyone else," and then we open up a conversation with them, and
then we open up a relationship, and that's how we go into the channel,
that's how we find our investors, that's how we go to market.
We make money for ourselves, most of the money is made by the investor.
We only make money for ourselves when the investor makes money, so
we give 80% of the return to the investor and we keep 20% for ourselves.
So we capture part of the value that we give our investors, we capture it
for ourselves. But if our investors don't make money, we don't make a
thing.
In addition, we co-invest. We take risk ourselves, so that's how we capture
value, that's how we make money. So sometimes, what will happen is I'll
be pitching an investor, but our competitor will be pitching the same
investor as well.
And so, people want the same investors and the same revenue streams
that we're trying to get, so we have to have ways to defend ourselves from
those investors, and the main way we do that is with our technological
ability. We explain to the investor, "You can certainly go with that other
guy, but we call what they do "hunt and peck", and we have precision
targeting, and we have our long track record of success because we apply
precision targeting and it works. And that's how we get the high yield, and
that's how we protect our investors with precision targeting.
So that's why I'd like you to invest in this business today. There are many
other people investing. As you can see, it's not difficult, with the level of
technology, and precision, and accuracy, and hard work, and experience
that we have, for us to find people. We don't want bad investors. Investors
aren't like customers, because, as William said, we're public, there's
tremendous security oversight.
So if you're a crappy investor, even if you give me the money, I suffer, so I
really, really have to screen you hard. First of all, I have lots and lots of
Pitch Teardown
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	13
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 15 --

good investors over here that are already in. I don't want to introduce a
bad investor to a good gene pool, so I'd like to hear from you right now,
what kind of investor you are and how you behave when you're in a deal,
and why we would like to take your money. So if you could reflect back to
me, I think that'd be a good place to take the conversation. And now I've
given you the complete pitch for William's business.
I wanted to share that with you so you could see when you have the
pitching skill...this is a business I've learned about, and I'm sure I got some
things wrong and I'm sorry about that. I'm not really sorry. See I can do
things wrong. I'm never sorry, why would I be? You know how much
effort...I'm not getting paid to do this, why should I be sorry?
Come up here and give you guys all this value, share everything I know. I
now know nothing. You guys know everything I know. If I'm going to
speak to you again, I've got to find some new stuff. I've come here on a
Saturday, left my family, spoken on stage, shared everything I know with
you guys, given you all my intellectual property, shared with you as much
as I could possibly do. It's not possible I could be sorry, I'm just happy I
could do that for you. I am.
Okay, so where do we go next? What I would like for you to do is reflect on
the pitch that we gave here and just to deconstruct it, we try to develop
some intrigue about this business. I just came up with this idea of us
against the earth. It's tension, it's intrigue. Find some kind of narrative.
Talk about the moving forces in the market, why you couldn't do this a
year ago and why it may be too risky to do a year from now, opened up a
market window.
But I haven't said we're the only one who knows how to do this, I've said
other guys are coming through the market window with us, and we
compete with them. But let me tell you how we compete. They do hunt
and peck and we do precision, and that's the difference between us.
And then I described to you our business model and who it's for and who
it's not for, so I think you could take those basic elements and continue to
refine your pitch.
Okay, so good. We're going to move into the next segment. I think this was
great and I do appreciate the energy that I feel back coming back from the
Pitch Teardown
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	14
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 15 --

room and the attention. Because, and this is the last thing I want to say,
your dream as a presenter, you walk into a room, you get on the phone,
you get on the internet, what you want is attention.
Because as I tell my guys, if I could lock you down in a chair, pin your eyes
open, pin your ears back, feed you coffee, and inject you with whatever
the government has to make people alert, I don't have to be that good, I
can sit here for four hours. I can sell whatever it is: blue plastic frogs,
mining, squirrel nut scratchers, rockets, birthday cakes you'll buy it all,
because you have to pay attention. You start to lose attention, we inject
you with some more stuff.
What you want is attention, that's what you're trying to get. And what I'm
hoping to say is, yes it's easy to get attention by lifting the volume very
high, but then you scare people. So the social tool is to get and keep
attention, and you guys have been giving me attention for a long time, and
that is something I do appreciate.
Pitch Teardown

-- 15 of 15 --
